script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Friday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed zero point four one percent up on the session. A narrow gain on a day where the tape was divided, with semiconductor strength pulling in one direction and broad tech pressure pulling in the other.

Nvidia surged more than four percent on Friday, closing above two hundred ten dollars for the first time in recent weeks. The catalyst was not Nvidia-specific — it came from across the aisle. Meta Platforms detonated a nearly six percent single-session gain after a cluster of AI infrastructure announcements reframed its capital spending narrative entirely. Chip names followed. The question now is whether this is a momentum reset or a one-day event.

No macro data was available for Friday's session. Markets appeared to trade on earnings anticipation and AI sentiment rather than hard economic prints. The broader tape was mixed, with select technology names leading while defensive and rate-sensitive sectors lagged. Nasdaq-level moves were driven primarily by large-cap AI infrastructure names outperforming the broader index composite.

The lead story of Friday's session belongs to Meta Platforms, and its direct implications for every semiconductor and AI infrastructure name in this portfolio. Shares of Meta Platforms closed at six hundred sixty-nine dollars and twenty-one cents, up thirty-seven dollars and seventy-three cents, a gain of five point nine seven percent on the session. The catalyst was a cluster of AI infrastructure and product announcements that materially reframed the company's heavy capital spending posture. According to reporting from Yahoo Finance, the market had previously treated Meta's aggressive AI investment as a pure expense drag on earnings. Friday's disclosures shifted that narrative — analysts began treating the same spending as a pathway to lower operational costs, new revenue streams, and faster growth compounding. That distinction matters enormously for Nvidia, Advanced Micro Devices, and the broader semiconductor complex represented in this portfolio. When the largest buyer of AI compute signals that its spending is generating returns, the demand visibility for chip suppliers improves. Meta is not a primary holding, but its session move was the single most consequential event for names that are.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, beginning with the largest position. Nvidia closed at two hundred ten dollars and ninety-six cents, up eight dollars and eighteen cents, a gain of four point zero three percent on the session. As noted, the Meta Platforms AI infrastructure announcements were the primary tailwind, lifting chip sentiment broadly. Investor Insights Business Daily flagged Nvidia as trading near a buy point, with Taiwan Semiconductor earnings on the immediate horizon serving as the next major catalyst for the name. Watch Taiwan Semiconductor's print closely — it will either confirm or complicate Nvidia's demand story.

Apple closed at three hundred fifteen dollars and thirty-two cents, down zero point two eight percent on the session. The headline was significant: Apple filed a lawsuit on Friday against OpenAI and two former Apple employees, alleging theft of trade secrets. Multiple sources confirmed the filing. The legal action injects near-term uncertainty around Apple's AI partnership structure with OpenAI, a relationship the market had largely treated as settled. Watch for any OpenAI response and whether the suit triggers renegotiation of their existing integration agreement.

Alphabet closed at three hundred fifty-seven dollars and eighteen cents, down one point seven one dollars, a loss of zero point four eight percent. No Alphabet-specific news drove Friday's move. The name underperformed relative to the AI infrastructure rally, which is notable given its deep exposure to the same thematic. Watch the upcoming earnings season, where Alphabet's cloud and search monetization numbers will be the key data points.

Micron Technology closed at nine hundred seventy-nine dollars and thirty cents, down twelve dollars and thirty-four cents, a loss of one point two four percent. Despite a brief mention in investor commentary around improved chip sentiment, Micron declined on the session even as peers rallied. Investor Insights Business Daily noted Micron is near a buy point heading into earnings. A pullback on a strong tape day for semiconductors is a data point worth tracking.

Microsoft closed at three hundred eighty-five dollars and ten cents, up zero point seven four dollars, a gain of zero point one nine percent. Microsoft was named by HSBC as one of ten stocks to watch as earnings season begins, per Yahoo Finance. The gain was modest — Microsoft did not participate meaningfully in Friday's AI-driven semiconductor rally. Its next major catalyst is its earnings release, where cloud growth and AI monetization metrics will be scrutinized.

Amazon closed at two hundred forty-five dollars and thirty-four cents, down one dollar and seventy cents, a loss of zero point six nine percent. Amazon Web Services was designated under direct financial oversight by the United Kingdom regulator, with the designation taking effect July thirteenth, per Yahoo Finance. This is a structural regulatory development for the cloud business. Monitor how AWS responds and whether additional regulatory jurisdictions follow the United Kingdom's lead.

Advanced Micro Devices closed at five hundred fifty-seven dollars and eighty-nine cents, up eleven dollars and seventeen cents, a gain of two point zero four percent. Advanced Micro Devices was a direct beneficiary of Friday's AI sentiment uplift driven by the Meta Platforms announcements. No company-specific news broke on the session. The name is tracking Nvidia's momentum closely heading into earnings season, where data center revenue will be the key line item to watch.

Tesla closed at four hundred seven dollars and seventy-six cents, up one dollar and twenty-one cents, a gain of zero point three zero percent. A New Jersey legislative bill that would ban self-driving vehicles not equipped with radar and lidar poses a direct regulatory risk to Tesla's robotaxi deployment plans in that state, per Yahoo Finance. Tesla gained modest ground on the session despite the headline. China market share data cited in the same report was characterized as positive. Watch the New Jersey bill's legislative progress.

Intel closed at one hundred nine dollars and eighty-four cents, down two dollars and seventy cents, a loss of two point four zero percent. Intel was the weakest primary holding on a day when semiconductor peers broadly rallied. No Intel-specific news was available. The divergence from the chip tape — particularly given Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices both gaining meaningfully — underscores the execution gap the market continues to price into Intel relative to its peers.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, Taiwan Semiconductor reports earnings imminently. As the primary manufacturer for Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices, its guidance on AI chip demand will set the tone for the entire semiconductor complex in this portfolio. Second, Goldman Sachs and GE Aerospace are also scheduled to report, per Investor Insights Business Daily — financial and industrial earnings will frame the broader risk appetite heading into next week. Third, the United Kingdom's AWS oversight designation takes effect July thirteenth, which falls within the immediate window. Any statement from Amazon or the regulator will move the cloud narrative.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Taiwan Semiconductor's earnings report is the single event that will most directly shape how this portfolio opens next week — do not miss it.
